title: LLM Inference: Batching, KV Cache, vLLM vs SGLang
description: The flagship module on serving LLMs in production. KV cache arithmetic, why decode is memory-bound, continuous batching, PagedAttention, RadixAttention, speculative decoding, quantization, and the 2026 inference stack.

sections.title: Why Inference Is the Bottleneck
sections.content:
Training a 70B model is a one-time capital expense. **Serving it is forever.** Inference is where the unit economics live, and it is fundamentally harder to make fast than training. Training is the easy regime: you have full batches of long sequences, every step is a dense matmul, the GPU runs at ~50% of peak BF16 TFLOPS, and life is good.

Inference is a different beast because **autoregressive decoding generates one token at a time**. That single fact rewires the entire performance story.

### Prefill vs Decode — Two Different Workloads

Every LLM inference request has two phases:

| Phase | What happens | Bottleneck | Tokens per step |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prefill** | Encode the prompt of length L in one pass | Compute (FLOPs) | L tokens in parallel |
| **Decode** | Generate one token at a time | Memory bandwidth | 1 token per step |

Prefill is a normal transformer forward pass. You feed in the entire prompt, run the full layer stack once, get the K/V cache and the first generated token. The matmuls are big (sequence dim × hidden dim), tensor cores run flat-out, and you saturate HBM->SM compute throughput. A H100 hits 80%+ MFU during prefill.

Decode is where it gets ugly. For each new token you must:

1. Load the full model weights from HBM into the SMs.
2. Compute attention over the entire KV cache (which gets longer every step).
3. Produce one output token.
4. Append to the KV cache.
5. Repeat.

The matmul shape during decode is `(1, hidden_dim) × (hidden_dim, hidden_dim)`. That's a **GEMV**, not a GEMM. You move 16 GB of weights for ~13 GFLOPs of work. The arithmetic intensity is roughly 1 FLOP per byte. The H100's HBM3 hits 3.35 TB/s; its tensor cores hit 989 TFLOPS FP16. That gives a balance point of ~295 FLOP/byte — and decode runs at 1. **You are using less than half a percent of the GPU's compute capacity during decode.**

### Throughput vs Latency — The Real Tradeoff

There are two metrics that matter and they pull in opposite directions:

- **TTFT (Time To First Token)**: how long until the first token appears. Dominated by prefill.
- **TPOT (Time Per Output Token)**: steady-state generation speed. Dominated by decode.
- **Throughput**: total tokens/sec across all concurrent users. Dominated by how many requests you can pack into one decode step.

A serving system optimized for throughput batches aggressively — more concurrent requests means each weight-load amortizes over more output tokens. A system optimized for low TTFT runs requests one-at-a-time so prefill never queues. Real serving stacks are forced to pick a point on this curve, and the rest of this module is about how vLLM, SGLang, TGI, and TensorRT-LLM each pick differently.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  P["Prefill\n(compute-bound, L tokens at once)"] --> D1["Decode step 1\n(memory-bound, 1 token)"]
  D1 --> D2["Decode step 2\n(memory-bound, 1 token)"]
  D2 --> D3["Decode step 3\n(memory-bound, 1 token)"]
  D3 --> Dn["...\n(repeat hundreds of times)"]
  Dn --> END["EOS"]
```

The rest of inference engineering is dedicated to making that long decode chain less wasteful: amortize the weight load across many requests (batching), avoid recomputing attention (KV cache), avoid memory waste (PagedAttention), and generate more than one token per memory cycle (speculative decoding).

sections.title: The KV Cache — Why It Exists and What It Costs
sections.content:
The KV cache is the single most important optimization in autoregressive decoding. Without it, generating token *t* requires re-running attention over all *t-1* previous tokens — quadratic blowup. With it, you cache the K and V projections of every past token and the per-step cost stays linear in *t*.

### Why It Works

In causal attention, the Q for the current token attends to the K and V of *all previous tokens plus itself*. The K and V of previous tokens are **deterministic functions of those tokens and the (frozen) weights** — they don't depend on what comes after. So once you've computed `K_j = W_K · x_j` and `V_j = W_V · x_j` for position *j*, you never need to recompute them. Store them and reuse.

For the current token at position *t* you compute a fresh `Q_t`, dot it against the cached `[K_1, ..., K_t]`, softmax, and combine with cached `[V_1, ..., V_t]`. The cost of generating one token is O(t × d) instead of O(t² × d).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph Layer["One transformer layer at decode step t"]
    X["x_t (current token embed)"] --> Q["Q_t = W_Q x_t"]
    X --> KN["K_t = W_K x_t"]
    X --> VN["V_t = W_V x_t"]
    KCACHE[("KV cache\nK_1..K_(t-1)\nV_1..V_(t-1)")] --> APPEND["append K_t, V_t"]
    KN --> APPEND
    VN --> APPEND
    APPEND --> KFULL["K_1..K_t, V_1..V_t"]
    Q --> ATTN["softmax(Q_t · K^T / √d) · V"]
    KFULL --> ATTN
    ATTN --> OUT["o_t"]
  end
```

### The Memory Cost

For every token in every layer in every head, you store one K vector and one V vector. The total cache size is:

```
kv_bytes = 2 × n_layers × n_kv_heads × d_head × T × bytes_per_element
```

Note `n_kv_heads`, not `n_heads`. Modern models use **GQA (Grouped Query Attention)** — multiple query heads share a single KV head — specifically to shrink this number. Llama-3-8B has 32 query heads but only 8 KV heads. DeepSeek V3 uses MLA, which compresses the cache further still.

### Worked Example: Llama-3-8B

Config: `n_layers=32, n_kv_heads=8, d_head=128, dtype=bf16 (2 bytes)`.

Per-token cost:

```
2 × 32 × 8 × 128 × 2 = 131,072 bytes = 128 KiB per token
```

For a single 8K context request: `8192 × 128 KiB = 1 GiB of KV cache`. For a 32K context: 4 GiB. Llama-3-70B with its bigger head dim and more layers is ~10x worse per token. On an 80 GB H100 hosting Llama-3-70B in BF16 (140 GB weights — already needs tensor parallel), you have maybe 60 GB left for KV after weights and activations. That caps your concurrent user × context-length product, which is exactly the metric that determines your serving cost.

### Why Naïve PyTorch KV Cache Wastes Memory

The textbook implementation looks like:

```python
# Pre-allocate maximum-length cache
kv_cache = torch.zeros(B, max_seq_len, 2, n_layers, n_kv_heads, d_head)
```

This is awful in production. Three reasons:

1. **You don't know the final length.** A request might generate 50 tokens or 5000. Allocating for `max_seq_len` (say 8192) for every slot means every request reserves the worst-case footprint up front.
2. **Fragmentation.** When request A finishes after 200 tokens and request B is just starting at 7000 tokens, you can't pack B's cache into A's freed slot — the cache lives in one contiguous block per slot.
3. **You can't oversubscribe.** Some requests will finish early; you'd love to use their memory for new requests immediately, but contiguous-allocation forces you to wait.

This is the exact problem PagedAttention solves — covered two sections down.

sections.title: Static Batching and Why It Falls Apart
sections.content:
The naïve way to serve multiple users is **static batching**: collect N requests, pad them to the same length, run them as one big batch through the model. Training stacks do this and it works fine because training sequences are pre-shuffled into uniform-ish length buckets.

For inference it's a disaster. Three reasons.

### Problem 1: Variable Output Lengths

Request A wants 10 tokens. Request B wants 500. In a static batch:

- You start both together.
- After step 10, A is done — but you can't return its result yet, because B is still generating.
- You either wait until step 500 (A's slot sits idle for 490 steps), or you cancel A and re-issue it next batch.

For a typical chat workload where 80% of replies are <100 tokens and 5% are 2000+, this means **the majority of your GPU-seconds are wasted decoding padding for finished requests**.

### Problem 2: Variable Prompt Lengths

The same thing in reverse, but at prefill time. If one request has a 32-token prompt and another has a 4000-token prompt, static batching pads everything to 4000, doing 125x extra prefill FLOPs for the short request and tanking TTFT.

### Problem 3: New Requests Block

With static batching, you commit to a batch and run it to completion. A new request arriving 2ms after the batch starts waits the *full duration* of the slowest request in the current batch before it can even begin prefill. For 70B models with chatty users that's tens of seconds of avoidable latency.

### A Picture of the Waste

```mermaid
gantt
    title Static batching — slot utilization over time
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Slot 0 (req A, 10 tokens)
    generating  :a1, 0, 10
    PADDING IDLE :crit, a2, 10, 500
    section Slot 1 (req B, 500 tokens)
    generating  :b1, 0, 500
    section Slot 2 (req C, 50 tokens)
    generating  :c1, 0, 50
    PADDING IDLE :crit, c2, 50, 500
    section Slot 3 (req D, 200 tokens)
    generating  :d1, 0, 200
    PADDING IDLE :crit, d2, 200, 500
```

The red bars are FLOPs you paid for and got nothing back. With realistic length distributions, GPU utilization sits around **20-30%** under static batching. The fix is iteration-level scheduling — covered next.

sections.title: Continuous Batching — The Orca Insight
sections.content:
The breakthrough idea, introduced in the **Orca paper** (OSDI 2022) and now used by every serious serving stack, is to **batch at the granularity of decode iterations, not requests**.

The insight: in autoregressive decoding, each step is independent — there's no reason to keep finished requests in the batch or to wait for new requests until the next "batch" starts. Every iteration, look at which slots are free, and admit a new request if there's one waiting.

### How It Works

The scheduler maintains a pool of *active* requests. Each decode step:

1. Build a batch of all currently-active requests (each at their own current position).
2. Run one decode iteration — one token per active request.
3. For any request that emitted EOS or hit max-tokens, **evict it** and return the result.
4. **Admit waiting requests** to fill any freed slots (subject to GPU memory headroom).
5. Repeat.

The word "continuous" means slots are refilled continuously, not in batches. Each request progresses at its own pace through prefill -> decode -> EOS, and the GPU stays full as long as the queue has work.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Q as Queue
    participant S as Scheduler
    participant GPU as GPU step
    loop every decode step
        S->>Q: any waiting?
        Q-->>S: req X waiting
        S->>S: free slot? (req A finished)
        S->>GPU: batch = [B, C, D, X(prefill)]
        GPU-->>S: tokens [b_t, c_t, d_t, x_0]
        S->>S: check EOS / max-tokens
    end
```

### The Prefill / Decode Mixing Problem

A subtle wrinkle: new requests need a prefill (which is compute-heavy and processes many tokens), while ongoing requests need a decode (which is memory-bound and processes one token). Mixing them in one batch hurts both — the prefill phase dominates kernel launch time and the decode requests sit idle waiting.

Different engines handle this differently:

- **vLLM** (pre-v0.6) ran prefill and decode as separate batches, alternating between them. This works but adds latency when both kinds of work coexist.
- **vLLM 0.6+ chunked prefill** breaks a long prefill into chunks small enough to co-batch with decode steps without blowing the iteration time.
- **TGI** runs prefill in a dedicated pass when capacity allows, otherwise interleaves.
- **SGLang** uses chunked prefill by default and overlaps with continuous decode batching.
- **DeepSeek's split-phase serving** (2025) goes one step further — separate prefill and decode GPUs entirely, prefill servers stream KV to decode servers. This is becoming the new SOTA for high-throughput deployments.

### Throughput Numbers

The Orca paper reported **23x throughput improvement** over a strawman batching baseline. In practice the realistic comparison is more like:

| Strategy | Llama-3-70B throughput (tok/s on H100×8) |
|---|---|
| Single request | ~30 tok/s |
| Static batch of 8 | ~80 tok/s (padding waste) |
| Continuous batching | ~600 tok/s |
| + PagedAttention | ~1400 tok/s |
| + speculative decoding | ~2800 tok/s |
| + FP8 quant | ~5000 tok/s |

Continuous batching alone is the largest single jump in serving throughput history. If you remember one thing from this module: **iteration-level scheduling is the foundation everything else stacks on**.

sections.title: PagedAttention — The Real Contribution of vLLM
sections.content:
vLLM's original 2023 paper is titled *"Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention"*. Note the title: **memory management**, not faster math. This trips people up. PagedAttention is not a faster attention kernel. It is a memory allocator. Its contribution is taking an idea from operating systems — paged virtual memory — and applying it to the KV cache.

### The Problem It Solves

With contiguous per-request KV allocation:

- **Internal fragmentation**: you allocate `max_seq_len` for each slot. If a request uses 500 tokens of an 8192-token budget, 7692 tokens of memory are reserved-but-unused.
- **External fragmentation**: when requests of variable length finish, you can't pack new requests into the freed regions if those regions aren't large enough contiguously.
- **Inability to share**: if two requests share a prefix (system prompt, few-shot examples), each gets its own copy of the cache. Wasteful.

The vLLM paper measured **20-40% of GPU memory wasted** on fragmentation alone in production deployments.

### The Idea: Blocks

Instead of allocating one contiguous chunk per request, divide GPU memory into fixed-size **blocks** (e.g., 16 tokens per block, ~2 MiB each for Llama-3-8B). Each request maintains a **block table** that maps logical token positions to physical blocks — exactly like a page table in an OS.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph Logical["Request A's logical KV (length 35)"]
    L0["tokens 0..15"] --> L1["tokens 16..31"] --> L2["tokens 32..34 (+padding)"]
  end
  subgraph Physical["Physical block pool on GPU"]
    B0["block 0"]
    B1["block 1 (Req B)"]
    B2["block 2 (Req A logical 0)"]
    B3["block 3 (Req A logical 2)"]
    B4["block 4 (free)"]
    B5["block 5 (Req A logical 1)"]
    B6["block 6 (Req C)"]
  end
  L0 -. block_table[A][0]=2 .-> B2
  L1 -. block_table[A][1]=5 .-> B5
  L2 -. block_table[A][2]=3 .-> B3
```

The block table indirection lets the engine:

1. **Allocate blocks lazily.** A new request reserves *one* block. When that fills up, allocate another. Memory grows token-by-token, not all-at-once.
2. **Reclaim instantly.** A finished request returns all its blocks to the free pool. No fragmentation — every freed block is interchangeable with every other.
3. **Share blocks across requests.** Two requests with the same system prompt point their first N block-table entries at the same physical blocks. The shared prefix is computed once and read by everyone.
4. **Copy-on-write.** Beam search with multiple candidates can share the prefix and only fork blocks where they diverge.

### The Cost

Paged attention requires a custom kernel — you can't just call `cublasGemm` because the K and V for a sequence aren't contiguous in memory. The attention kernel reads the block table, gathers blocks, and computes attention over a logically-but-not-physically contiguous sequence. The vLLM paper showed this kernel has 2-4% overhead vs. contiguous attention — vastly outweighed by the memory wins.

### The Numbers

The original vLLM paper measured **2-4x throughput improvement over HuggingFace TGI** (which at the time used contiguous KV) on Llama-2-13B with ShareGPT prompts. That comparison has narrowed since TGI added paged attention itself, but PagedAttention remains the largest single memory-management win in inference history.

### The Takeaway

If you ever see PagedAttention described as "a faster attention algorithm," the author hasn't read the paper. It is a **block-level KV cache allocator** that eliminates fragmentation, enables prefix sharing, and lets you serve more concurrent requests per GB. The attention kernel itself is essentially the same.

sections.title: vLLM vs SGLang vs TGI vs TensorRT-LLM
sections.content:
The 2026 inference landscape has four serious players, each with a distinct design philosophy. This is opinionated — pick what matches your workload.

### vLLM

The default. Open-source (Apache 2.0), pure-Python with custom CUDA kernels, deployed everywhere. PagedAttention is its signature, continuous batching is solid, ecosystem support is unmatched (every new model lands in vLLM within days of release).

**Strengths**: huge model coverage, mature OpenAI-compatible API server, strong community, good defaults. As of v0.6 with chunked prefill and the v1 architecture, throughput is competitive with anything.

**Weaknesses**: Python overhead in the scheduler shows up at very high request rates. Prefix caching exists but is less aggressive than SGLang's RadixAttention. Tensor-parallel inference has some sharp edges.

**Pick vLLM when**: you want a one-line `pip install vllm` deployment, your workload is general chat with mixed prompts, you need to swap models often.

### SGLang

The newcomer (2024) that ate vLLM's lunch on workloads with shared prefixes. SGLang's contribution is **RadixAttention**: instead of just KV-cache-blocks, it stores the cache in a **radix tree** keyed by token sequences. Any request that shares a prefix with a cached one reuses the entire cached subtree automatically — no manual prefix marking required.

**Why this is a big deal**: in real workloads, prefix sharing is everywhere. System prompts. Few-shot examples. RAG-retrieved chunks reused across queries. Conversation history in multi-turn chat. SGLang catches all of these automatically. Reported speedups of **5-10x throughput** on prefix-heavy workloads vs. vLLM.

**Strengths**: best-in-class prefix caching, fast scheduler, strong on multi-turn chat and agent workloads, growing model support.

**Weaknesses**: smaller ecosystem than vLLM, fewer model architectures supported (closing fast). Less battle-tested at extreme scale.

**Pick SGLang when**: your workload has shared prefixes (RAG, agents, multi-turn chat, structured generation), and you want to squeeze every drop of throughput.

### TGI (Text Generation Inference)

HuggingFace's serving stack. Rust core for the scheduler and HTTP layer, CUDA kernels for the model. Heavily integrated with the HF Hub.

**Strengths**: Rust scheduler has lower overhead than Python-based competitors at very high QPS. Excellent Docker-and-Kubernetes story. Good safety features (request timeouts, queue limits, prometheus metrics out of the box). Strong for enterprise deployments where ops matters.

**Weaknesses**: development pace has slowed; vLLM and SGLang ship new optimizations faster. Less aggressive on the bleeding-edge tricks.

**Pick TGI when**: you're already in the HF ecosystem, you need production-grade ops (metrics, tracing, K8s), and your workload is steady-state rather than chasing peak throughput.

### TensorRT-LLM

NVIDIA's official compile-once-replay-many stack. You compile your model into a TensorRT engine ahead of time, baking in batch size, sequence length, quantization, and parallelism choices. The result runs at very close to peak hardware throughput.

**Strengths**: **the fastest** for fixed-shape, high-throughput, latency-sensitive deployments. INT4 AWQ, FP8, custom kernels, and the deepest H100/H200/B200-specific optimizations live here first. TensorRT-LLM + Triton Inference Server is the standard "we own a fleet of H100s and ship at scale" stack.

**Weaknesses**: build times are long (minutes to hours per engine config). You compile for a specific (batch, seqlen, TP-size) tuple — flexibility costs you. Model support trails vLLM by weeks to months. Learning curve is steep.

**Pick TensorRT-LLM when**: you're running one or two well-known models at very large scale, you can afford ops complexity, you need every last token/sec.

### Side-by-side

| | vLLM | SGLang | TGI | TensorRT-LLM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Language | Python+CUDA | Python+CUDA | Rust+CUDA | C++/CUDA |
| KV management | PagedAttention | RadixAttention | Paged (v2+) | Paged + custom |
| Prefix cache | Manual / token-level | Automatic (radix tree) | Yes | Yes (limited) |
| Chunked prefill | v0.6+ | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Speculative decoding | Yes (medusa, eagle, draft) | Yes | Yes | Yes (best support) |
| Quantization | FP8, INT4-AWQ, GPTQ | FP8, INT4-AWQ | bitsandbytes, AWQ, GPTQ | FP8, INT4-AWQ, INT8-SQ |
| Compile overhead | None (JIT) | None | None | Hours per engine |
| New model lead time | ~Days | ~1-2 weeks | ~Weeks | ~Weeks to months |
| Best for | General serving | Prefix-heavy / agents | Production HF stack | Peak-throughput at scale |

### Honorable Mentions

- **MLC-LLM**: cross-platform (browser, mobile, edge). Different niche entirely.
- **llama.cpp / llama-server**: the CPU/MPS/edge story. Not for GPU serving at scale.
- **DeepSpeed-MII**: Microsoft's stack. Used internally; less external traction.
- **LMDeploy**: Shanghai AI Lab's stack, strong in China; competitive throughput numbers.

sections.title: Speculative Decoding — Cheating the Memory Wall
sections.content:
Decode is memory-bound: you spend 99% of your time loading weights from HBM. Once those weights are loaded, you have free FLOPs left over. **Speculative decoding** uses those free FLOPs to verify multiple candidate tokens per memory cycle, multiplying throughput.

### The Algorithm

Leviathan et al. (Google, 2022) and Chen et al. (DeepMind, 2023):

1. Have a **draft model** — small, fast, same vocabulary as the big model (e.g., a 1B alongside a 70B).
2. Generate K candidate tokens cheaply with the draft model: `d_1, d_2, ..., d_K`.
3. Feed all K candidates into the big model **in parallel** — one forward pass, K+1 logit positions.
4. Compare: for each position *i*, accept `d_i` if the big model's distribution agrees "enough" (exact criterion: importance sampling — accept with probability `min(1, p_big(d_i) / p_draft(d_i))`).
5. On the first rejection at position *r*, replace `d_r` with a token re-sampled from the big model's distribution and discard `d_{r+1}..d_K`.
6. You generated `r+1` tokens in one big-model forward pass.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  D["Draft model\n(1B params, fast)"] -->|generates 5 candidate tokens| C["d_1 d_2 d_3 d_4 d_5"]
  C --> V["Big model verifies\nin ONE forward pass"]
  V -->|accept until first rejection| O["e.g., d_1 d_2 d_3 + resampled d_3' = 4 tokens"]
```

### Why It Works

The big model can compute logits for K positions in essentially the same wall time as logits for 1 position — because the bottleneck is HBM weight loading, not compute. The K extra forward-pass slots are nearly free.

If the draft accepts K' tokens on average (K' < K), and the draft model is roughly *free* relative to the big model, the effective throughput multiplier is **K' + 1**. Typical numbers:

| Model pair | Avg accept length | Speedup |
|---|---|---|
| Llama-3-70B + Llama-3-8B draft | ~3.5 / 5 | ~2.5x |
| Medusa heads on 70B | ~2.7 / 4 | ~2.0x |
| EAGLE-2 on 70B | ~4.5 / 6 | ~3.0x |

### Variants

- **Vanilla draft model**: separate small model, same tokenizer. Simplest. The draft's quality directly determines acceptance rate.
- **Medusa**: instead of a separate model, **bolt extra prediction heads onto the big model itself** to predict *t+1, t+2, t+3...* in parallel. Trained on top of frozen weights. No second model needed.
- **EAGLE / EAGLE-2**: feature-level autoregression — predict next *features* (not just tokens), then decode. Higher acceptance rates than Medusa, slightly more complex.
- **Lookahead decoding**: no draft model at all; uses Jacobi iteration to generate candidates from the big model's own context. Lower speedup (~1.5x) but trivial to deploy.

### Where It Helps and Where It Doesn't

**Speculative decoding helps decode, not prefill.** Prefill is already compute-bound — the GPU is at 80%+ MFU running real matmuls. There are no free FLOPs to spend on speculation. Adding draft+verify to prefill makes it *slower*, not faster. Modern engines disable speculative decoding during prefill and enable it only during decode.

It also helps less when:

- **Batch size is high**: at batch=32, decode is no longer memory-bound — the big model is already amortizing weight loads. Free FLOPs disappear. Speedup drops to ~1.2-1.5x.
- **Draft model is poor**: low acceptance rate kills the speedup. You're doing extra work for tokens you reject.
- **Sampling temperature is high**: random sampling diverges from the draft more often. Greedy decoding gets the biggest speedup.

### Real-world Numbers

- DeepSeek V3's MTP (multi-token prediction) training trick is essentially Medusa baked into pretraining. It hits **~1.8x decode throughput** with 85%+ acceptance on the second predicted token.
- vLLM with EAGLE-2 on Llama-3-70B reports **2.5-3x decode TPS** at batch size 1.
- TensorRT-LLM with medusa-3-heads on Llama-3-8B: **2.1x** at batch=1, dropping to **1.3x** at batch=32.

sections.title: Quantization — The Other Multiplier
sections.content:
Inference quantization reduces the precision of model weights (and sometimes activations and KV cache), trading a small quality hit for a large memory and bandwidth win. Since decode is memory-bound, **halving weight size roughly doubles decode throughput**. This is the second-largest serving optimization after batching.

### The Format Zoo

| Format | Bits | Where it lives | Quality cost | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FP16 / BF16** | 16 | Weights + activations | None (baseline) | Default training/inference precision. |
| **FP8 (E4M3 or E5M2)** | 8 | Weights + activations (H100+ only) | <1% perplexity on most models | Hardware-native on H100/B200. The current production sweet spot. |
| **INT8** | 8 | Weights only or W+A | 1-3% degradation depending on calibration | Works on older hardware (A100, T4). |
| **INT4 AWQ** | 4 | Weights only | 2-5% degradation, model-dependent | Activation-aware weight quantization. Strong default for INT4. |
| **INT4 GPTQ** | 4 | Weights only | 2-6% degradation | Post-training quantization with hessian-based calibration. |
| **INT2 / 1.58-bit** | 2 / ~1.6 | Weights only | Significant; requires QAT to be usable | Mostly research as of 2026; not production. |

### Calibration vs Naïve Cast

Naïve quantization: `int_weight = round(fp_weight / scale)` where `scale` is chosen per tensor to fit the range. This is what `bitsandbytes` 8-bit does in its simplest mode. It works for INT8 with mild quality loss.

It **does not work** for INT4. The dynamic range of model weights is too high; uniform quantization wastes precision on outlier-dominated tensors.

The two methods that do work:

- **GPTQ** (Frantar et al. 2022): per-layer, finds the quantization that minimizes a layer-wise reconstruction error using the inverse Hessian of activations on a small calibration set. Sequential layer-by-layer.
- **AWQ** (Lin et al. 2023): observes that ~1% of weights (those activated by large input features) carry most of the signal. AWQ scales those weights up before quantization and rescales the inputs to compensate, protecting the important channels. Typically beats GPTQ at INT4 by 0.5-1 perplexity.

Both need a calibration dataset (~128-1024 sample sequences). Naïve INT4 without calibration loses 5-15 perplexity points on Llama models — unusable.

### Quantizing the KV Cache

Weights aren't the only thing in HBM. KV cache memory traffic dominates decode at long contexts. Modern engines support **KV cache quantization**:

- FP8 KV cache: drops the cache by 2x with ~0.5% quality cost. Default in vLLM/SGLang on H100.
- INT4 KV cache: 4x reduction; some quality loss, depends on per-channel scaling. Worth it for very long contexts.

### How Quantization Stacks With Batching

This is the part people miss. Quantization's win comes from **less HBM traffic per token**. That win is largest in the memory-bound regime — batch size 1, low concurrency. As batch size grows and decode becomes compute-bound, the FP8/INT4 speedup shrinks.

On an H100 with Llama-3-70B:

| | BF16, batch=1 | FP8, batch=1 | BF16, batch=64 | FP8, batch=64 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Decode TPS per request | 14 | 28 | 11 | 17 |
| Effective speedup | — | 2.0x | — | 1.55x |

FP8 still wins at high batch — the tensor cores on H100 also run FP8 at 2x the BF16 TFLOPS — but the gap narrows. The shape of this curve is why production engines auto-tune quantization based on workload.

### When to Pick What

- **FP8** on H100/H200/B200: the default. Almost no quality cost, real throughput win, hardware support is mature.
- **INT8 W8A8** on A100 or older: when FP8 isn't available. Acceptable quality on calibrated checkpoints.
- **INT4 AWQ**: when memory is the constraint — fitting a 70B model on a single A6000 (48GB) or two consumer GPUs. Quality cost is real but tolerable for most tasks.
- **INT4 GPTQ**: similar use case to AWQ; pick whichever has better calibration for your model. AWQ tends to win for Llama-family.
- **Don't mix W4A16 with high batch sizes**: the dequantize-on-the-fly cost in compute-bound regimes is wasted. Use W4A4 or W8A8 if both weight and activation need quantization.

sections.title: The Full 2026 Inference Stack
sections.content:
Putting it all together. The state-of-the-art recipe for serving a 70B-class LLM in 2026:

```
  PagedAttention KV cache
+ Continuous batching with chunked prefill
+ Prefix caching (RadixAttention-style if workload has shared prompts)
+ Speculative decoding (Medusa or EAGLE)
+ FP8 weights + FP8 KV cache (H100/B200)
+ Tensor parallel (TP=2 or 4 depending on model size)
+ CUDA Graphs for decode (eliminates per-step kernel-launch overhead)
+ Possibly: disaggregated prefill / decode servers (DeepSeek-style)
```

Each element multiplies. The compounding looks roughly like:

| Stack | Llama-3-70B TPS on 8×H100 |
|---|---|
| BF16, batch=1, no tricks | ~30 |
| + continuous batching @ batch=32 | ~700 |
| + PagedAttention | ~1400 |
| + FP8 weights + FP8 KV | ~2800 |
| + CUDA Graphs | ~3200 |
| + speculative decoding (EAGLE) | ~5000 |
| + RadixAttention on prefix-heavy workload | ~10000-15000 (workload-dependent) |

The difference between "first principles" and "production" is two orders of magnitude. **Engineering matters.**

### CUDA Graphs

One piece not yet covered: **CUDA Graphs**. Each decode step launches ~100-300 CUDA kernels (one per attention, matmul, norm, activation across all layers). Each kernel launch has ~5-10 µs of CPU-side overhead. At 1000 tok/s that's 100-300 µs/step × 1000 = 100-300 ms/sec of wall time spent in launch overhead alone.

A CUDA Graph captures the entire sequence of kernel launches as a single recorded object, then replays it with one launch. Per-step overhead drops from 100-300 µs to ~2 µs. The catch: the captured graph is shape-specific. Engines pre-capture graphs for a set of common batch sizes / KV cache lengths, and pick the closest one at runtime, padding if necessary. This is one of the reasons production-grade engines (TensorRT-LLM, vLLM with `--enforce-eager=False`) decisively beat hand-written PyTorch baselines.

### Disaggregated Prefill / Decode

DeepSeek's 2024-2025 deployment innovation: **separate the prefill GPUs from the decode GPUs**. Prefill is compute-bound, wants big tensor-core utilization, scales well with longer prompts. Decode is memory-bound, wants high memory bandwidth per request and low tail latency. Mixing them on the same GPU forces compromises.

The split: prefill servers handle incoming prompts at high MFU, stream the resulting KV cache over fast interconnect (NVLink, IB) to a decode server. The decode server runs continuous batching at very high concurrency since it never gets disrupted by prefill. Reported gains: **+30-50% throughput** at the same TTFT budget. As of 2026 this is becoming the default for hyperscale deployments.

### Picking the Stack for Your Workload

| Workload | Recommended stack |
|---|---|
| Single-tenant chatbot, low QPS | vLLM, FP8, EAGLE, batch=1-4 |
| Multi-tenant chat, mixed prompts | vLLM or SGLang, continuous batching, PagedAttention, FP8 |
| RAG with shared retrieved chunks | **SGLang** for RadixAttention prefix sharing — easily 3-5x on top of vLLM |
| Agent workloads (long shared system prompts) | **SGLang**, RadixAttention is built for this |
| Production at hyperscale, single model | **TensorRT-LLM** + Triton, FP8, EAGLE, CUDA graphs, optionally disaggregated |
| Edge / on-device | llama.cpp, INT4 quant, MLC-LLM if cross-platform |
| Research / experimentation | HuggingFace transformers (slow but flexible), or vLLM offline mode |

### What Not to Do

A few common mistakes:

- **Running `transformers.generate()` in production.** It's a reference implementation; expect 10-50x worse throughput than vLLM on the same hardware.
- **Picking INT4 weight quantization at batch 128.** Decode becomes compute-bound at high batch; the dequant cost eats the memory savings.
- **Disabling KV cache to "save memory."** You'll do O(T²) attention work per token. Don't.
- **Speculative decoding at batch=64.** The free-FLOP regime is gone; you're paying for tokens you reject. Disable it.
- **Static batching with `pad_to_max_length=8192` in HF Trainer for serving.** This is the workload everyone benchmarks against to make their own engine look 50x faster.

### Closing Thought

LLM inference performance is a stack, not a single trick. The biggest production wins come from getting all the layers right: scheduler (continuous batching), memory (PagedAttention + paged KV), throughput (speculative decoding), precision (FP8), and runtime (CUDA Graphs). Drop any one and the others give you less than they should.

The field moves fast. As of mid-2026: vLLM v1 has stabilized, SGLang has consolidated its position on agent workloads, TensorRT-LLM dominates closed-shop hyperscale, and the next frontier is **disaggregated serving and serverless KV** — treating the cache as a network-attached resource rather than a per-GPU concern. The constants will change. The principles in this module — memory-boundness of decode, value of iteration-level scheduling, paged allocation, compute headroom for speculation — will not.

quiz.question: Why is decode memory-bound while prefill is compute-bound on a modern GPU?
options: Prefill uses tensor cores; decode does not.

Decode loads the full model weights from HBM but only does ~1 FLOP per byte loaded (a GEMV with batch 1). Prefill processes many tokens in parallel, so each weight load amortizes over hundreds of FLOPs, saturating the tensor cores.
Decode uses sparse attention; prefill uses dense.
Decode runs on the CPU.
quiz.explanation: The arithmetic intensity of decode with batch=1 is ~1 FLOP/byte. H100 HBM3 is 3.35 TB/s; tensor cores are 989 TFLOPS. The compute/bandwidth balance point is ~295 FLOP/byte. Decode runs at <0.5% of peak compute — HBM bandwidth is the only thing that matters. Prefill is the opposite: many tokens × hidden dim means each weight load drives hundreds of FLOPs, and tensor cores run at 80%+ MFU.

quiz.question: Llama-3-8B has 32 layers, 8 KV heads, head dim 128, served in BF16. Roughly how much KV cache does ONE request with an 8K context consume?
options: About 128 MB.
About 1 GB.
About 8 GB.
About 32 GB.
quiz.explanation: Per-token: 2 (K and V) × 32 layers × 8 KV heads × 128 head dim × 2 bytes = 131,072 bytes ≈ 128 KiB. For 8192 tokens: 8192 × 128 KiB = 1 GiB. This is why GQA (8 KV heads instead of 32) and MLA (DeepSeek's compression) exist — the KV cache, not the weights, is what limits concurrent users at long contexts.

quiz.question: Static batching pads all sequences to the longest one and runs them together. What's the dominant problem this causes in production inference?
options: Padding tokens corrupt the attention output.
Requests with short outputs occupy their batch slot doing wasted decode work until the slowest request finishes — so most GPU-seconds are spent on padding. New requests cannot start mid-batch.
GPU memory becomes fragmented.
The model produces wrong answers.
quiz.explanation: If request A wants 10 tokens and request B in the same batch wants 500, A's slot sits idle (still being computed but discarded) for 490 steps. New requests can't enter the batch until everything finishes. With realistic length distributions, GPU utilization sits at ~20-30%. Continuous batching (Orca, 2022) fixes this by scheduling at iteration granularity — finished requests are evicted and new ones admitted every decode step.

quiz.question: PagedAttention's main contribution is best described as:
options: A faster attention math kernel that reduces FLOPs.
A new attention pattern that beats softmax attention.

A block-level KV cache allocator (analogous to OS paged virtual memory) that eliminates fragmentation, allows lazy growth, and enables prefix sharing — the attention kernel itself is essentially unchanged.
A compression scheme for KV cache values.
quiz.explanation: The vLLM paper is titled *Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving*. PagedAttention is a memory allocator, not a math optimization. It divides KV cache into fixed-size blocks, indexes them with a per-request block table, allocates lazily, reclaims instantly, and enables cross-request sharing. The custom attention kernel that reads non-contiguous blocks has ~2-4% overhead vs contiguous attention — vastly outweighed by the memory savings (20-40% in production).

quiz.question: When does SGLang's RadixAttention beat vLLM's PagedAttention most strongly?
options: Single-tenant inference with one request at a time.

Workloads where many requests share token prefixes — RAG with reused chunks, agent loops with shared system prompts, multi-turn chat. The radix tree auto-detects prefix overlap and reuses the cached KV subtree, giving 3-10x throughput over vLLM on prefix-heavy workloads.
Workloads with very long generations and short prompts.
Mobile/edge deployments.
quiz.explanation: RadixAttention stores KV cache in a radix tree keyed by token sequences. Any request whose prefix matches a cached path reuses the entire cached subtree — no manual prefix specification needed. This is enormous for RAG (shared retrieved chunks across users), agents (shared system prompt + tool definitions), and multi-turn chat (conversation history). On workloads without shared prefixes, SGLang and vLLM are comparable; on prefix-heavy workloads, SGLang reports 5-10x gains.

quiz.question: Why does speculative decoding help most at low batch sizes and stop helping at high batch sizes?
options: Draft models don't support large batches.

Speculative decoding cheats the memory wall by using free FLOPs that exist when decode is memory-bound. At high batch sizes (~32+) decode is no longer memory-bound — weight loads are amortized across the batch, the big model is already compute-busy, and there are no free FLOPs to spend verifying speculation. The 'extra' work of verifying becomes real work.
The draft model becomes slower at high batches.
Memory runs out at high batches.
quiz.explanation: Speculative decoding pays for K extra logit positions per big-model forward pass, on the bet that decode is memory-bound and those extra FLOPs are nearly free. At batch=1 this is true; at batch=64 the big model is already saturating tensor cores from the batch, and the extra K positions cost real compute time. Acceptance-rate-based throughput multipliers shrink from ~2.5x at batch=1 to ~1.2-1.3x at batch=32, sometimes to <1x — at which point you disable it. Same reason speculative decoding doesn't help prefill: prefill is already compute-bound.

quiz.question: You're serving Llama-3-70B on 2×H100. You need to fit it in memory and you'll run mostly batch=1 chat at low concurrency. What quantization makes the most sense?
options: INT4 GPTQ for both weights and activations — maximum compression.

FP8 (H100 native). Weight + KV cache in FP8 gives ~2x decode throughput with <1% quality cost, hardware tensor cores accelerate FP8 matmuls at 2x BF16. INT4 would give more memory savings but more quality cost; with 2×H100 you have memory headroom — pick FP8.
BF16 with no quantization — quality first.
INT2 — newest is best.
quiz.explanation: FP8 is the production sweet spot on H100/B200. Native hardware support means tensor cores run FP8 at 2x BF16 TFLOPS, weight memory drops 2x, KV cache memory drops 2x — and the quality cost is sub-1% on standard benchmarks for Llama-class models. INT4 AWQ is for memory-constrained deployments (single GPU 70B). With 2×H100 you don't need INT4; the quality saved is worth it. INT2 is research-grade, not production. Always-BF16 leaves a free 2x on the table.

quiz.question: You're building a RAG service. Most queries retrieve from a corpus of ~1000 chunks; the same 50-100 chunks get reused heavily across users. Each query is: [system prompt][retrieved chunks][user question]. What inference stack should you pick?
options: vLLM with PagedAttention.

SGLang with RadixAttention. The prefix structure (system prompt is identical; retrieved chunks repeat across queries) is exactly what RadixAttention's radix-tree prefix matching is designed to exploit. Expect 3-5x throughput over vLLM at similar latency. Add FP8 + chunked prefill on top.
TensorRT-LLM with INT4 AWQ.
Plain HuggingFace transformers.
quiz.explanation: RAG with reused chunks is the canonical RadixAttention win case. Every cached chunk's KV is reused automatically without any application-side prefix management. vLLM's prefix cache exists but is less aggressive — RadixAttention's tree handles arbitrary multi-prefix overlap. TensorRT-LLM is faster per-token but doesn't have automatic radix-style prefix sharing, and its compile overhead doesn't suit a workload where many prompt variants flow through. Always benchmark on your real prompts; the answer changes when prefix-sharing rate is low.

exercise.title: Build a Toy Continuous Batching Scheduler
exercise.description:
Simulate an LLM serving system with N independent requests, each with a random prompt length and target output length. Implement two schedulers — static batching (wait for the slowest in batch) and continuous batching (replace finished slots immediately) — then measure the throughput and latency gap.

**Setup.** Each request *r* has:
- `prompt_len_r`: tokens of prompt (prefill cost).
- `output_len_r`: tokens to generate (decode cost).
- `arrival_step_r = 0`: all requests are present at t=0 (keeps the simulation simple).

The "GPU" has BATCH_CAP slots. Each simulation step processes one decode iteration: every active slot produces one token. For prefill we'll approximate cost as `prefill_steps = ceil(prompt_len / PREFILL_THROUGHPUT_PER_STEP)` blocking steps during which the slot is busy but no output is emitted.

**Part A — Static batching (given).** Read and run the provided `run_static(...)`. The scheduler fills the batch once with the first BATCH_CAP requests, runs decode steps until ALL are done, and only then admits the next batch.

**Part B — Continuous batching (your job).** Implement `run_continuous(...)`. Each step: check which slots are free (just finished), pull the next waiting request into that slot (start with its prefill steps), then advance every busy slot by one token (or one prefill step). Continue until all requests are done.

**Part C — Measure.** Print a comparison table: total wall-time steps, throughput (output tokens / step), mean latency per request (steps from t=0 to its completion), p95 latency.

You should see continuous batching achieve ~2-3x throughput and dramatically lower mean latency, especially with high output-length variance. No torch, no numpy — pure Python with `random`.
exercise.starterCode:
import random
import math
from collections import deque

random.seed(42)

# --- Simulation parameters ---
N_REQUESTS = 128
BATCH_CAP = 8
PREFILL_THROUGHPUT_PER_STEP = 256  # tokens of prompt prefilled per step in a slot
MIN_PROMPT, MAX_PROMPT = 50, 2000
# Skewed output-length distribution (most short, a few very long) — the
# realistic chat workload. This is where static batching falls apart.
def sample_output_len():
    # 80% short replies, 15% medium, 5% long
    r = random.random()
    if r < 0.80: return random.randint(10, 120)
    if r < 0.95: return random.randint(120, 500)
    return random.randint(800, 1500)

# --- Generate workload ---
def make_workload(n):
    reqs = []
    for i in range(n):
        reqs.append({
            'id': i,
            'prompt_len': random.randint(MIN_PROMPT, MAX_PROMPT),
            'output_len': sample_output_len(),
        })
    return reqs

workload = make_workload(N_REQUESTS)
print(f'Workload: {N_REQUESTS} requests, mixed short/long output (chat-like)')
total_output_tokens = sum(r['output_len'] for r in workload)
print(f'Total output tokens to generate: {total_output_tokens}\n')

# --- Slot state helpers ---
def new_slot(req, current_step):
    return {
        'req': req,
        'prefill_remaining': math.ceil(req['prompt_len'] / PREFILL_THROUGHPUT_PER_STEP),
        'tokens_emitted': 0,
        'admitted_step': current_step,
    }

def step_slot(slot):
    """Advance one slot by one GPU step. Returns True if the request just finished."""
    if slot['prefill_remaining'] > 0:
        slot['prefill_remaining'] -= 1
        return False
    slot['tokens_emitted'] += 1
    return slot['tokens_emitted'] >= slot['req']['output_len']

# --- Part A: STATIC batching (provided) ---
def run_static(workload):
    queue = deque(workload)
    completion_step = {}
    step = 0
    while queue:
        batch = [queue.popleft() for _ in range(min(BATCH_CAP, len(queue)))]
        slots = [new_slot(r, step) for r in batch]
        while any(s is not None for s in slots):
            for i, s in enumerate(slots):
                if s is None:
                    continue
                finished = step_slot(s)
                if finished:
                    completion_step[s['req']['id']] = step
                    slots[i] = None
            step += 1
    return step, completion_step

# --- Part B: CONTINUOUS batching (YOUR TURN) ---
def run_continuous(workload):
    queue = deque(workload)
    slots = [None] * BATCH_CAP
    completion_step = {}
    step = 0
    # TODO:
    # 1. Each step: fill any empty slot from the queue (assign new_slot).
    # 2. Advance every non-None slot by one step.
    # 3. If a slot finishes, record completion_step[req_id] = step and set slot to None.
    # 4. Stop when queue empty AND every slot is None.
    # Hint: be careful to admit BEFORE stepping, and to record completions correctly.
    return step, completion_step

# --- Part C: Measure and compare ---
def report(name, total_steps, completions):
    latencies = [completions[r['id']] for r in workload]
    throughput = total_output_tokens / total_steps
    mean_lat = sum(latencies) / len(latencies)
    p95 = sorted(latencies)[int(len(latencies) * 0.95)]
    print(f'{name:<25} steps={total_steps:>5}  thru={throughput:>6.2f} tok/step  '
          f'mean_lat={mean_lat:>5.1f}  p95_lat={p95}')

s_steps, s_done = run_static(workload)
report('Static batching', s_steps, s_done)

c_steps, c_done = run_continuous(workload)
if c_done:
    report('Continuous batching', c_steps, c_done)
    print(f'\nThroughput speedup: {s_steps / c_steps:.2f}x')
else:
    print('Continuous scheduler not implemented yet.')
exercise.solution:
import random
import math
from collections import deque

random.seed(42)

N_REQUESTS = 128
BATCH_CAP = 8
PREFILL_THROUGHPUT_PER_STEP = 256
MIN_PROMPT, MAX_PROMPT = 50, 2000

def sample_output_len():
    r = random.random()
    if r < 0.80: return random.randint(10, 120)
    if r < 0.95: return random.randint(120, 500)
    return random.randint(800, 1500)

def make_workload(n):
    return [{
        'id': i,
        'prompt_len': random.randint(MIN_PROMPT, MAX_PROMPT),
        'output_len': sample_output_len(),
    } for i in range(n)]

workload = make_workload(N_REQUESTS)
print(f'Workload: {N_REQUESTS} requests (chat-like length skew)')
total_output_tokens = sum(r['output_len'] for r in workload)
print(f'Total output tokens: {total_output_tokens}\n')

def new_slot(req, current_step):
    return {
        'req': req,
        'prefill_remaining': math.ceil(req['prompt_len'] / PREFILL_THROUGHPUT_PER_STEP),
        'tokens_emitted': 0,
        'admitted_step': current_step,
    }

def step_slot(slot):
    if slot['prefill_remaining'] > 0:
        slot['prefill_remaining'] -= 1
        return False
    slot['tokens_emitted'] += 1
    return slot['tokens_emitted'] >= slot['req']['output_len']

# Static batching
def run_static(workload):
    queue = deque(workload)
    completion_step = {}
    step = 0
    while queue:
        batch = [queue.popleft() for _ in range(min(BATCH_CAP, len(queue)))]
        slots = [new_slot(r, step) for r in batch]
        while any(s is not None for s in slots):
            for i, s in enumerate(slots):
                if s is None:
                    continue
                if step_slot(s):
                    completion_step[s['req']['id']] = step
                    slots[i] = None
            step += 1
    return step, completion_step

# Continuous batching — the solution
def run_continuous(workload):
    queue = deque(workload)
    slots = [None] * BATCH_CAP
    completion_step = {}
    step = 0
    while queue or any(s is not None for s in slots):
        # 1. Admit waiting requests into any free slots
        for i in range(BATCH_CAP):
            if slots[i] is None and queue:
                slots[i] = new_slot(queue.popleft(), step)
        # 2. Advance every active slot by one GPU step
        for i, s in enumerate(slots):
            if s is None:
                continue
            if step_slot(s):
                completion_step[s['req']['id']] = step
                slots[i] = None
        step += 1
    return step, completion_step

def report(name, total_steps, completions):
    latencies = [completions[r['id']] for r in workload]
    throughput = total_output_tokens / total_steps
    mean_lat = sum(latencies) / len(latencies)
    sorted_lat = sorted(latencies)
    p50 = sorted_lat[len(sorted_lat)//2]
    p95 = sorted_lat[int(len(sorted_lat) * 0.95)]
    print(f'{name:<25} steps={total_steps:>5}  thru={throughput:>6.2f} tok/step  '
          f'mean_lat={mean_lat:>5.1f}  p50={p50:>4}  p95={p95}')

print('=' * 78)
s_steps, s_done = run_static(workload)
report('Static batching', s_steps, s_done)

c_steps, c_done = run_continuous(workload)
report('Continuous batching', c_steps, c_done)
print('=' * 78)
print(f'\nThroughput speedup: {s_steps / c_steps:.2f}x')
print(f'Step savings:      {(s_steps - c_steps) / s_steps * 100:.1f}%')

# Why does continuous batching win?
# Under static batching, every slot in the batch is held until the SLOWEST request
# in that batch finishes. A request that wants 10 output tokens is held for the
# 600 steps of the longest neighbor — its slot does decode work for 590 wasted steps.
# Under continuous batching, that slot is reclaimed immediately and a fresh request
# is admitted. Every slot is doing useful work essentially every step.
#
# Mean latency drops dramatically because short requests no longer wait behind long ones
# from earlier batches that haven't finished yet. Total throughput (tokens per step)
# rises proportionally to the fraction of waste that static batching had.
#
# In real LLM serving, the same logic compounds with PagedAttention (which removes
# the memory-fragmentation reason static batchers exist), speculative decoding (which
# multiplies tokens-per-step), and FP8 (which shrinks the per-token cost). The toy
# simulation captures the *core* scheduling win — typically 2-3x in this setup,
# matching the gap measured between vLLM (continuous) and naive HF generate (static).
